visual_checks: Check every form field — does it have a visible text label that describes what input is expected?
Look for form fields that rely only on placeholder text as a label (placeholder disappears on focus)
Check if required fields are clearly indicated (asterisk with explanation, 'Required' text, or aria-required)
Look for fields with specific format requirements — are instructions provided (e.g., 'Date: MM/DD/YYYY')?
Check groups of related fields (radio buttons, checkboxes) — is there a group label explaining the question?
Verify that labels are positioned near their associated fields (above or to the left for LTR languages)
Check if instructional text for complex forms is provided before the form or at the start of each section
pass_conditions: All form fields have visible, descriptive labels that remain visible when the field has focus
Labels are programmatically associated with their inputs via <label for='id'>, aria-labelledby, or aria-label
Required fields are clearly identified (text label, asterisk with explanation, or aria-required='true')
Fields with specific format requirements provide instruction text (e.g., 'Phone: (555) 123-4567')
Groups of related controls (radio buttons, checkboxes) have a group label via <fieldset>/<legend> or aria-labelledby
Complex forms provide instructional text at the beginning or at section headers
fail_conditions: Form fields have no visible labels — only placeholder text that disappears on input
Labels exist but are not programmatically associated with their form fields
Required fields are not identified — users must guess which fields are mandatory
Fields expecting specific formats (date, phone, SSN) provide no format instructions
Groups of radio buttons or checkboxes have no group label explaining the question being answered
Labels are ambiguous or generic (e.g., multiple fields all labeled 'Enter value')
Instructions are provided only after the form field, so screen reader users encounter the field before the instructions
na_conditions: Page contains no forms or input fields
Page has only display content with no user input
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Form fields are dynamically rendered and were not present in the captured DOM
Cannot verify label associations without inspecting the HTML source
common_mistakes: Using placeholder attribute as the sole label — it disappears when the user starts typing, removing the label
Floating labels that animate from inside the field to above it — these can cause issues if the CSS animation fails or if the label is not programmatically linked
Asterisks (*) marking required fields without an explanation that * means 'required'
Date fields without format instructions — users do not know if they should enter MM/DD/YYYY or DD/MM/YYYY
Password fields without complexity requirements listed before or during input
Labels that are visually hidden and only accessible via screen reader (sighted users have no label)
Input fields adjacent to each other with labels that could apply to either field (ambiguous association)

examples.pass: A registration form has visible labels above each field ('First Name', 'Last Name', 'Email Address'), each linked with <label for='...'>, required fields show '(required)' after the label text, and the password field has 'Must be at least 8 characters with one uppercase letter' below it.
examples.fail: A login form has two input fields with only placeholder text 'Username' and 'Password' — when the user clicks into a field, the placeholder disappears and there is no visible label to remind them what the field is for. Neither field has a <label> element.
examples.partial: Most form fields have proper visible labels, but the phone number field uses only a placeholder 'Phone' with no visible label and no format instructions, and the group of radio buttons for 'Preferred contact method' has no <fieldset>/<legend>.

auditor_anti_patterns: FALSE POSITIVE: flagging a form field as 'missing label' when any of these are present: a <label for='fieldId'> pointing to the field, the field is nested inside a <label>, an aria-label attribute, aria-labelledby pointing at a label element, or a title attribute. Walk every source before flagging.

FALSE POSITIVE: flagging a form field for 'missing instructions' when the instructions ARE present as visible text immediately before or after the field in the DOM (a

, , or

sibling). 3.3.2 does not require aria-describedby -- visible proximate instructions count.

FALSE POSITIVE: flagging <input type='hidden'>, <input type='submit'>, or <input type='button'> for 'missing label' -- hidden inputs are not user-facing and submit/button inputs use their value attribute as their label.

FALSE POSITIVE: flagging a search box that has a <label> with class='visually-hidden' or class='sr-only' for 'missing visible label' -- visually-hidden labels are a W3C-recommended pattern for search fields with adjacent icon context. 3.3.2 requires a label, not necessarily a sighted-visible one.
plain_meaning: Every control that asks the user for input must come with a label or instructions that make clear what should be entered. The label can be a visible text label, an aria-label, an aria-labelledby reference, a nested label element, a visually-hidden but programmatically-present label, or clear adjacent instructions. The key is that a programmatic label or instruction exists.
scope_applies_to: All form input elements (text, email, password, number, etc.)
Checkboxes and radio buttons (individual and grouped via fieldset/legend)
Custom form widgets that collect user input
Required-field indicators and format instructions where needed
scope_does_not_apply_to: Icon-only buttons and controls outside forms (those fall under SC 4.1.2)
Accessible name of buttons that submit a form (the button label itself is SC 4.1.2)
Visual placement or styling of labels as long as a programmatic label exists
borderline_calibration: A visually-hidden (sr-only) label IS a valid label for this SC. Do NOT flag controls that have a programmatic label just because the label is not visible to sighted users.
Any of label[for], nested label, aria-label, aria-labelledby, title, or clearly adjacent instructional text counts as providing a label.
A placeholder alone is NOT a sufficient label but a placeholder plus adjacent text that together identify the purpose can be acceptable.
Flag only when NO programmatic labeling mechanism of any kind is present for a user-input control.
If a control has any accessible name via any standard mechanism, do not flag it here.
